Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I work part-time jobs to cover my expenses, so I had to squeeze studying into whatever gaps I could find between shifts. I'm a hands-on learner, so I tried to do practice problems and actually work through things physically rather than just reading. Most weeks I'd aim for around 20 hours of study, but it rarely happened at consistent times. I'd often study late at night or early morning when I could actually focus without worrying about money or work.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was going on in my life. When deadlines hit, I'd cram everything into a few intense sessions, which honestly stressed me out even more. Some weeks I barely touched anything because I had to pick up extra shifts to pay rent. I tried to be regular about it, but life kept throwing curveballs at me. The inconsistency made it really hard to build any momentum in the course.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was due or when I got an email notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance was so low. The gamified task notifications actually caught my attention a few times—they felt different from regular course stuff. But honestly, a lot of times I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind, and then just close it again.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got into a rhythm where checking the LMS felt natural. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I tried to make it a daily habit, but then work schedules changed or anxiety got bad, and it fell apart. The gamified elements were interesting and almost got me hooked, but I couldn't sustain it. It always felt like another thing on my already overflowing plate.
Q5: The video content was actually helpful when I could sit down and watch it—I logged about 80 minutes of watch time. I learn best by seeing things done and then trying them myself, so videos showing practical applications worked for me. The discussion posts were useful too because hearing other students' perspectives helped things click. I also appreciated when there were interactive elements that let me actually do something rather than just passively absorb information.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials, honestly. Long text documents just don't work for my brain—I need to be doing something with my hands or watching demonstrations. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary resources because I was already struggling to keep up with the core material. Some of the forum discussions I skipped because by the time I logged in, the conversation had moved on and I felt too behind to contribute meaningfully.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate practical application or if it helped me understand something I was stuck on. The gamified tasks actually surprised me—they made even small accomplishments feel like progress, which I desperately needed. When something connected to real-world skills I could use, I was more motivated. But if it felt like busywork or something I'd never use, my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With everything else going on—work, stress, trying not to fail—I had to prioritize required stuff even if I didn't always complete it. Required activities carried this weight of consequence that made them feel more urgent but also more anxiety-inducing. Optional ones sometimes seemed interesting, but I'd think 'I can't even handle the mandatory stuff, why add more?' It's frustrating because some optional content might have actually helped me understand things better.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and videos when I had wifi, then study them on the bus to work or during breaks. I borrowed textbooks from the library and worked through problems by hand in notebooks. I also watched YouTube videos explaining concepts because sometimes outside sources explained things in ways that clicked better for my learning style. Most of my actual learning probably happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through practice problems in a notebook for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd rewatch downloaded lecture videos multiple times, pausing and rewinding to really understand, but that wouldn't register. Sometimes I'd meet with a classmate to work through concepts together—we'd spend hours on it—but the system only saw that I hadn't logged in. My kinesthetic learning means I need to physically do things, and that rarely shows up digitally.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd access a video once, download it, and then watch it three or four more times offline over the next week. Same with lecture slides—I'd print them out at the library and annotate them by hand. My internet at home isn't always reliable, and I can't afford unlimited data, so downloading once and studying offline was just practical. The LMS probably thinks I barely engaged with that content when really I was studying it intensively.
Q12: There were several really hard periods. Midway through the semester, my hours at work increased and I was exhausted all the time. My anxiety was already high, and falling behind made it spiral worse. There was this vicious cycle where I'd feel too anxious to log in, then I'd fall further behind, which made the anxiety worse. Some weeks I was just in survival mode—getting through each day was the goal, not academic success.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from back-to-back shifts, I couldn't focus on anything complex. Confusion about course material made me avoid the LMS because logging in just reminded me how lost I was. Financial stress was constantly in the background—hard to care about discussion posts when you're worried about making rent. All of these things created this barrier where even opening the LMS felt like too much emotional energy.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or replaying stressful conversations from work. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but nothing was actually sinking in. There were nights I'd stare at readings for an hour and realize I hadn't absorbed a single thing. My body was present but my brain had checked out from exhaustion and anxiety.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew it would hurt me. The anxiety around starting them was sometimes paralyzing—I'd think about all the work involved and just freeze. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was less tired or stressed, but tomorrow never came with better conditions. It's not that I didn't care; I cared so much that the fear of doing it wrong stopped me from doing it at all. That's how I ended up with such low completion rates.
Q16: Deadlines were both motivating and terrifying. They'd force me to finally engage, but usually too late to do quality work. I'd have these panic sessions right before something was due, cramming in as much as possible. The LMS probably shows spikes of activity around deadlines and silence in between. Honestly, deadlines added to my stress more than they helped structure my learning. I wish I could have spread things out more evenly.
Q17: When I did get feedback, it mattered a lot to me. Positive feedback gave me hope that maybe I could turn things around. Critical feedback was harder—sometimes it made me want to try harder, but other times it confirmed my fears that I was too far behind. The problem was feedback often came when I'd already mentally moved on or was drowning in other responsibilities. Timing made a big difference in whether I could actually use it.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming for someone in my situation. Having 15 online courses meant content was scattered everywhere and hard to track. The pacing assumed I had consistent time to dedicate, which I didn't. I appreciated when modules were clearly organized, but even then, catching up after falling behind felt impossible. A more flexible structure with clearer priorities would have helped me focus my limited energy better.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made engagement easier—they broke things into smaller, less intimidating pieces and gave me small wins. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the assumption that everyone had equal time and resources. The lack of hands-on, practical activities was tough for my learning style. Also, when participation in discussions was expected but I'd missed weeks of context, jumping in felt awkward and pointless.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. High activity might mean someone is trying—logging in, looking at things, attempting to engage—but struggling to actually complete work. Maybe they're confused and searching for help, or anxious and checking obsessively without producing anything. For me, sometimes activity was just me trying to figure out what I'd missed or feeling guilty and logging in without having capacity to actually do the work. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That's interesting—it's kind of the opposite of my situation. I think some students might be efficient, doing focused work offline and only using the LMS minimally to submit. Or they might have strong background knowledge and not need all the resources. I tried to be like that with offline studying, but my exam scores show it didn't translate to performance. Maybe those students have fewer external stressors and can actually focus during the limited time they spend online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, how long they spend, what they click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything happening offline. They can't see that I watched a downloaded video five times or spent hours with a notebook working through problems. They can't capture my anxiety levels or that I was working two jobs. The logs show behavior but not context, effort, or genuine understanding. They're a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning and putting in genuine effort, even when it's hard. It's not just showing up—it's wrestling with concepts until they make sense, asking questions, trying to connect new knowledge to what you already know. For me, engagement also means not giving up even when everything feels impossible. I was engaged in my own way, even if my numbers don't show it. I wanted to succeed; I just couldn't always make it happen.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually wanted to be a good student, or the guilt I felt every time I fell behind. It can't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the anxiety that made logging in feel impossible some days. It doesn't know about my work schedule, my financial stress, or how hard I was trying to climb out of a difficult situation with limited resources. My engagement was real—it just happened mostly outside the system and was constantly fighting against circumstances beyond my control.
